student_message: That makes so much sense! If I fold a long envelope diagonally, the edges will definitely stick out. What if a shape is a triangle like a yield sign, how can it fold perfectly with an odd number of sides?
final_message:
You are thinking like a true mathematician! 🌟 

To fold a yield sign perfectly, we do not fold a side to a side. Instead, we start right at one of the pointy corners. If you draw a line straight down the middle from that top point to the flat edge at the bottom, you create a line of symmetry! When you fold the triangle on that line, the left and right halves will hug each other to make a perfect mirror image. 

Because all $$3$$ sides of a yield sign are the exact same length, you can actually fold it from any of its three corners to the flat side across from it. Isn't it neat how folding from a point to a flat edge makes the symmetry work perfectly? 📐

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain how a yield sign triangle folds perfectly by folding through a corner to the opposite side.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is engaged, happy, and asking a conceptual question about the symmetry of triangles with an odd number of sides. The dialogue agent is best suited to explain this concept.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: Zara covered rotational and reflectional symmetry, including vertical, horizontal, and diagonal fold lines in letters ('V', 'X') and shapes. She understands these concepts exceptionally well, accurately identifying multiple symmetry lines. Zara exhibited no struggles. Her learning state is highly engaged and progressive; having mastered basic 2D symmetry, she proactively asks advanced questions, such as how symmetry applies to 3D objects and why diagonal symmetry fails when squares are stretched into rectangles.
